name: shell-escape-command
description: Driver types Claude Code's !cmd shell-escape (`!echo hello`) in the interactive REPL. claude runs the shell command locally without an LLM round-trip and writes <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrapper events to the transcript. Per parser.go:216-225, irrlicht's claudecode parser deliberately skips these wrapper events, so state must stay in ready throughout (no synthesized turn). The recipe then sends a normal user prompt to prove the session is still alive and a real prompt still drives the ready→working→ready arc. Verdict adopts the intent reading of the spec (no stuck in working) per assessment.json — the alternative literal reading would require a parser change to synthesize a transition arc.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

### Agent supports the primitive (yes)

The scenario's **Primitive exercised** is a shell-escape mechanism — "Claude Code `!cmd`, or analogous." Aider implements the exact same primitive: the in-chat `/run` command runs a shell command locally (no LLM round-trip) and `!` is its documented shorthand alias. Per aider's in-chat command reference, `/run` is "Run a shell command and optionally add the output to the chat (alias: !)". So aider's `!echo hello` is a direct one-to-one analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape this scenario is modeled on — not a loose stand-in. (https://aider.chat/docs/usage/commands.html)

### daemon_capability (full) — the daemon emits every spec-required observable

The **User-observable signal** the scenario asserts is exactly: "`ready → working → ready`; state does not stick in `working` after the shell command exits." The scenarios.json `verify` block adds `tool_calls_max: 0`. Both are met by the daemon AS IT EXISTS TODAY.

The transport is `FilesUnderCWD{Filename: ".aider.chat.history.md", RawLineParser}` (core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/agent.go:45-51). For `/run echo hello`, aider writes a `#### /run echo hello` user-prompt line into the markdown history; the parser's `####` handler (parser.go:94-108) opens a turn → `working`. ~1500ms after the file goes quiet, `IdleFlush` synthesizes `turn_done` (parser.go:216-229) → `working → ready`, clamped behind the SessionDetector ~5s working-refresh poll. Net lifecycle: `ready → working → ready`, never stuck in `working`, never `waiting`. That is precisely the asserted signal.

On the tool-count axis: the live aider 0.86.2 recording shows aider 0.86.2 does NOT write a `> Running <cmd>` blockquote for `/run` — it writes `> Add command output to the chat? (Y)es/(N)o` followed by `> Added N lines of output to the chat.`, which fall through the parser's generic ignored-blockquote branch (parser.go:126-133). So `runningRE` (`^>\s*Running\s+`, parser.go:69-70) never matches, `p.toolCall("Bash")` (parser.go:122-124) never fires, and ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted. The spec's `tool_calls_max: 0` therefore HOLDS for aider (zero tool calls observed) — for a different reason than claudecode (which deliberately skips its `<bash-input>`/`<bash-stdout>` wrappers), but the spec-required observable is satisfied either way.

Every Expected: signal the canonical spec requires — the state arc, no stick in working, no waiting, zero tool calls — is represented by the daemon today. There is no spec-required observable the daemon mis-handles, so this is `daemon_capability: full`, not `bug`. And the behavior leaves a clear trace in the watched Source (the `#### /run …` line drives a real turn), so it is not `incapable`.

### Why this is `full` + a caveat, not `bug` (#476 re-derivation)

The old schema rated this `irrlicht_observes: partial`. That `partial` conflated two facts: (a) the spec-required state arc + zero-tool assertions are fully met (a `full` fact), and (b) a NON-spec property — irrlicht cannot tell a `/run` shell-escape apart from an ordinary user-prompt turn, since the escape surfaces only as the surrounding state arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event. Fact (b) is exactly the "feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant" caveat pattern: the canonical spec does NOT require a distinct shell-escape event (it asserts the arc + final ready + zero tools), so the indistinguishability is a caveat, NOT a `bug`. Per the authoring rule, caveats over downgrades — keep `daemon_capability: full` and surface the gap as a yellow callout.

### driver_capability (ready)

The recipe's steps are `slash` (`/run echo hello`), `sleep`, `send` (the final normal prompt), `wait_turn`, `sleep`. The aider interactive driver (scripts/drive-aider-interactive.sh) implements send / slash / wait_turn / interrupt / sleep. `/run echo hello` is an inline-arg slash (delivered verbatim via tmux send-keys -l), NOT an arrow-key picker, so no `keys` step is needed. Every step type the recipe needs exists → `driver_capability: ready`. None of the driver's known gaps (restart, reset_session, resume, start_session, exit_clean, sigkill, keys, session, fork) is on this recipe's path.

### Capability key

No dedicated `shell_escape` key exists in any adapter's capabilities.json. The `/run`/`!` surface is gated by `slash_commands: true`; the observation channel would conventionally be `tool_calls: true`, but the live aider 0.86.2 recording shows the escape emits NO tool call (no `> Running` line → runningRE never fires), so the escape is observed via the surrounding `ready → working → ready` state arc rather than through `tool_calls`. Both keys are `true` for aider (capabilities.json:18,11).

## Routing

supports=yes, daemon=full, driver=ready → route: record. The recorded spec asserts the state arc + final `ready` + zero tool calls / no tool_result/Bash marker.
caveats: FEATURE-INVISIBLE-BUT-SPEC-COMPLIANT: aider 0.86.2's `/run` writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output`, NOT a `> Running <cmd>` line, so runningRE (parser.go:69-70) never fires and zero Bash tool_results are emitted. irrlicht therefore cannot distinguish the shell-escape from an ordinary user-prompt turn — it is observed only as the ready→working→ready arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event. The canonical spec does not require a distinct event, so this is a caveat, not a daemon defect.

The scenario's claudecode-shaped verify.tool_calls_max:0 DOES hold for aider 0.86.2 (zero tool calls observed), but for a different reason than claudecode: aider writes no parseable tool line at all, vs claudecode deliberately skipping its <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrappers.

Working→ready latency is idle-driven (IdleFlush after ~1500ms, clamped behind the SessionDetector ~5s working-refresh poll), so the ready transition trails the shell command's exit by several seconds rather than firing on the command's completion line.

TEARDOWN-RACE: the final working→ready IdleFlush settle must land before the tmux kill or the recording ends mid-working and the final ready + process_exited are lost. The recipe uses a ≥12s trailing sleep for this reason; in practice the FINAL turn's settle still races process death (reproduced across 4 recordings, sleeps 12/18/20s) — the assessed latency carve-out, marked known_failing in expected.jsonl. This is a latency artifact, not a spec-required observable the daemon mis-handles.

sources.note: In-chat commands: '/run — Run a shell command and optionally add the output to the chat (alias: !)'. Confirms aider's !cmd shell-escape is the direct analog of Claude Code's !cmd. Anchors agent_supports: yes.

The `####` user-prompt handler (L94-108) opens the turn → working; IdleFlush turn_done (L216-229) settles → ready — the full ready→working→ready arc the spec asserts. runningRE ^>\s*Running\s+ (L69-70) → toolCall("Bash") (L122-124) fires ONLY for a literal `> Running` line; aider 0.86.2's `/run` writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output`, which fall through the generic ignored-blockquote branch (L126-133), so no Bash tool_result is emitted (zero tool calls). Grounds daemon_capability: full + the feature-invisible caveat.

TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell (L162-172): a LITERAL '> Running echo hello' line produces exactly one Bash ToolUse — pins the runningRE→Bash path. aider 0.86.2 does NOT write that line for `/run` (it writes the `> Add command output…` confirmation pair), so this path is not exercised by a real `/run` escape; the live recording shows zero tool calls.

Source = FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser} (L45-51) — the markdown transcript that carries the `#### /run …` line the parser keys on. One Parser per process; no PendingContributor/SubagentCounter. Confirms the trace exists in the watched Source (not incapable).

No shell_escape key; slash_commands:true (L19) gates /run, tool_calls:true (L11) is the conventional observation channel but the /run escape emits no tool call. Maps the 'shell-escape mechanism' primitive to existing keys (Step 4 confirmation).

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a model out-of-band (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL plus the matching provider key/endpoint — e.g. a local LM Studio / OpenAI-compatible server). Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs on the model-auth prompt; the live REPL needs a model that actually answers the final normal prompt.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so the shell-escape and the final prompt can both be issued to one long-lived session.
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record.

OBSERVES:PARTIAL (per assessment.json — irrlicht_observes: partial): aider's `/run <cmd>` (alias `!<cmd>`) is the direct analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape (agent_supports: yes), but irrlicht does NOT surface it as anything distinct from an ordinary turn. OBSERVABILITY CORRECTION (live aider 0.86.2 recording, supersedes the earlier doc-based `> Running`-fold prediction): aider 0.86.2's `/run` does NOT write a `> Running <cmd>` line — it writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output` confirmation blockquotes, so runningRE never fires and ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted. The claudecode-shaped top-level verify.tool_calls_max:0 therefore DOES hold for aider 0.86.2 (zero tool calls), but for a different reason than claudecode. This cell asserts the ready→working→ready arc + final ready and asserts NO tool_result/Bash marker — the escape is observed only as the state arc; the absence of a distinct shell-escape event is the documented partial scope, not a defect.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in the transcript before sending the first step.

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears. There is no transcript-UUID rollout session — the whole arc lives on the one proc-<PID> row.

The shell-escape is issued via a `slash` step typing the literal text `/run echo hello` + Enter (the aider driver handles `slash` identically to `send` via tmux send-keys -l, so the inline argument is delivered verbatim; `/run` is an inline-arg slash, NOT an arrow-key picker, so no `keys` step is needed). `/run echo hello` writes a `#### /run echo hello` user-prompt line (opens a turn → working) then aider 0.86.2 writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output` confirmation blockquotes — NOT a `> Running` line — so runningRE never fires and NO Bash tool_result is emitted (OBSERVES:PARTIAL correction). NO wait_turn follows the escape — it produces no `> Tokens:` line, so wait_turn would time out; a trailing sleep lets IdleFlush settle working→ready instead.

aider has NO turn-end transcript marker — end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1.5s quiet, surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's ~5s SessionDetector working-refresh poll (so the working→ready settle trails the command exit by several seconds; this is why the post-escape sleep is ≥7s).
After the final wait_turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession (proc-<PID>) event in ready within ~2s of REPL launch (aider's transcript is lazy, so the pre-session is the launch-time observable)

the /run echo hello shell-escape drives a ready→working→ready arc on the bindable session: the `#### /run echo hello` line opens the turn (→working), aider runs the command locally, and IdleFlush settles the turn back to ready (~5s effective latency) — the escape does NOT stick in working and never enters waiting

NO-TOOL_RESULT (the observes:partial scope, observability correction vs the earlier doc-based prediction): aider 0.86.2 emits NO Bash tool_result for the `/run` escape. It writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output`, NOT a `> Running` line, so runningRE never matches and zero tool calls are recorded. The escape is observed only as the ready→working→ready state arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event. The top-level verify.tool_calls_max:0 happens to hold (zero tool calls) but for a different reason than claudecode; this is documented scope, not drift, and NO tool_result/Bash marker is asserted.
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the bindable aider session_id

after the normal user prompt, events.jsonl shows a second ready→working→ready arc on the SAME session_id — proves the session is still alive and a real prompt still drives state after the shell-escape
no state_transition to waiting appears for the session at any point
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the session at REPL teardown (one long-lived session); final session state is ready
recipe.notes: aider vs claudecode/codex/pi for this scenario: aider's `/run <cmd>` (documented alias `!<cmd>`) runs a shell command locally with no LLM round-trip — the direct analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape (assessment.json agent_supports: yes). The recipe is templated from the claudecode shell-escape recipe but diverges on the observation shape (assessment.json irrlicht_observes: PARTIAL). claudecode's parser deliberately SKIPS the <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrappers so the escape synthesizes NO turn and state stays ready throughout (its top-level verify.tool_calls_max:0 holds). OBSERVABILITY CORRECTION (live aider 0.86.2 recording — supersedes the earlier doc-based prediction that aider folds the escape into a Bash tool_result): aider 0.86.2's `/run` does NOT write a `> Running <cmd>` blockquote, so runningRE (^>\\s*Running\\s+, parser.go:69-70) never matches and p.toolCall("Bash") (parser.go:122-124) never fires — ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted. aider 0.86.2 instead writes `> Add command output to the chat?` + `> Added N lines of output` confirmation blockquotes, which fall through to the generic ignored-blockquote branch. (parser_test.go TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell still pins the runningRE→Bash path for a LITERAL `> Running` line, but `/run` never produces that line.) What still holds: the `####` user-prompt line aider writes for `/run` opens a real turn (→working), and ~1500ms later IdleFlush synthesizes turn_done (working→ready, clamped behind the SessionDetector ~5s poll). So the observable is a genuine ready→working→ready arc (NOT claudecode's stay-in-ready) with NO tool call — the escape is observed only as that arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event; that indistinguishability is exactly why the cell is partial. verify.tool_calls_max:0 happens to hold for aider 0.86.2 (zero tool calls), but for a different reason than claudecode. RECIPE SHAPE: the escape comes FIRST (via `slash` — the aider interactive driver supports send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep but NOT keys; `/run echo hello` is an inline-arg slash so `slash` delivers it verbatim, no `keys` gap), with NO wait_turn (it produces no `> Tokens:` line) followed by a ≥7s sleep so IdleFlush's working→ready lands before the next prompt re-enters working; then a normal `send`+`wait_turn` proves the session is still alive and a real prompt still drives the arc; a ≥12s trailing sleep holds the recording open past the final IdleFlush settle so the tmux kill doesn't race the final working→ready emission + process_exited (TEARDOWN RACE: an 8s tail let the kill land mid-working and lost the final ready + process_exited; ≥12s gives the ~5s SessionDetector poll + IdleFlush full room to settle). SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL surfaces a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch; the whole arc lives on that one proc-<PID> row (no transcript-UUID rollout for FilesUnderCWD). In expected.jsonl, escape_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin (matcher picks the FIRST working — the pre-session never goes working), and pid_bind / escape_end / final_turn_start / final_turn_end / teardown pin via same_session_as so a co-occurring pre-session can never satisfy them. Between runs: the shell command stdout, exact final-turn assistant wording, per-turn token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/parser.go (the `####`→turn-open at parser.go:94-108 and the IdleFlush turn_done at parser.go:216-229) and whether an aider update changed how `/run` / `!` escapes are written into .aider.chat.history.md (e.g. a return of the `> Running` line, which would re-introduce a Bash tool_result). KNOWN FAILING (5/7, live aider 0.86.2): the load-bearing escape arc (escape_start→escape_end: the /run shell-escape does NOT stick in working) + session_birth/pid_bind/final_turn_start all PASS, but final_turn_end + teardown FAIL — aider does not reliably emit the FINAL turn's working→ready settle before the tmux kill. When the final turn's last transcript write coincides with the working transition (no later fswatcher activity to re-arm the tailer), the SessionDetector refresh-ticker IdleFlush (~5s poll) races process death and the settle is lost. Reproduced across 4 recordings (trailing sleeps 12/18/20s; both file-editing and plain-reply final prompts). This is the assessed irrlicht_observes:partial latency carve-out, not a daemon regression and not recipe-fixable; marked known_failing in expected.jsonl. PROMPT NOTE: the final prompt is "Reply with exactly the word: ok" (mirrors basic-turn), but gpt-5-nano sometimes still interprets the word as a filename and edits a file — harmless to the arc; the teardown gap is independent of whether the final turn edits.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape is a TUI-only affordance: the user types `!ls` at the prompt and Claude Code runs the command in a local shell, then writes a record into the transcript wrapped in `<bash-input>` / `<bash-stdout>` content blocks. Crucially this runs WITHOUT an LLM round-trip — no API call, no `assistant` message, no `turn_duration`. The transcript simply gains a user-event whose `message.content` is the wrapper string.

### The parser deliberately ignores these events

`isLocalCommandContent` in parser.go:216-225 returns true for content prefixed with `<local-command`, `<command-name>`, `<bash-input>`, or `<bash-stdout>`. `handleUserEvent` (parser.go:169-187) checks this filter and sets `ev.Skip = true`, so the shell-escape user event is dropped before it reaches the metrics layer. No `LastEventType` update, no `HasOpenToolCall` flip, no fresh activity flag. From the classifier's point of view the session is quiescent — it stays in whatever state it was already in.

This is the right behaviour for the spec's first bullet: a shell-escape does NOT cause `ready → working`. Wait — the spec actually asks for `ready → working → ready`. Re-reading: 'Agent executes a shell command outside the tool framework (Claude Code `!cmd`, or analogous mechanisms in other adapters). The command runs and exits.' The spec wants the state arc to PASS THROUGH working, presumably because the dashboard should reflect that the session is briefly busy.

There is a tension here. Two readings are defensible:

**Reading A (literal):** The spec demands `ready → working → ready`. Current parser behaviour skips the event entirely, so the session stays `ready` throughout. Under reading A, irrlicht_observes is `no` — we never enter working at all.

**Reading B (intent):** The spec's deeper assertion is the second bullet: 'State does not stick in `working` after the shell command exits.' That is, the failure mode the spec guards against is the session getting stuck in working. Staying ready throughout trivially satisfies the no-stuck guarantee. Under reading B, irrlicht_observes is `yes` — we never even risk getting stuck because we never transition.

I'm going with reading B (yes) for three reasons:

1. The parser skip is intentional — `<bash-input>` was added to the skip-list specifically because these events are not real user turns (parser.go:213-225 comment).
2. Treating a non-LLM local-shell action as a turn would create flicker every time the user does `!ls` to check pwd — exactly the class of bug PR #106 was trying to eliminate.
3. The spec's expected list is followed by a single failure-mode bullet ('does not stick in working'); the first bullet is structural prose. A liberal-but-honest read aligns with intent.

That said, the verdict is sensitive to spec interpretation. If reviewers prefer reading A, the cell becomes `daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready` with `confidence: 0.9` and a recommendation to either (a) loosen the spec to 'no stuck working' or (b) add a synthetic `ready → working → ready` for shell-escape events. The recipe should make this an explicit test point.
caveats: Verdict is sensitive to spec interpretation: under a strict reading of `ready → working → ready` as a required state arc, irrlicht_observes flips to `no` because the parser skips `<bash-input>` events entirely. I'm assessing yes under the failure-mode-intent reading. The Stage-2 spec author should resolve this before the Stage-3 recipe locks the arc in expected.jsonl.

If the spec demands a literal transition arc, the fix is a one-line parser change: emit a `synthetic_shell_escape` event_type instead of skipping, with no terminal stop_reason, so rule 4 flips to working and the absence of further activity is handled by — wait, there's no inactivity sweep. So a literal-arc fix requires both emitting the event AND adding a synthetic-turn-done after the bash-stdout marker. That's a bigger lift than this cell warrants on its own.
Other adapters' analogous mechanisms (Codex `!`, aider `/run`, opencode shell mode) need their own assessment. The Claude Code answer doesn't generalize.

sources.note: Lines 216-225 (`isLocalCommandContent`): explicit skip-list `<local-command`, `<command-name>`, `<bash-input>`, `<bash-stdout>`. Lines 169-187 (`handleUserEvent`): sets `ev.Skip = true` for these, dropping them from the metrics pipeline before they touch state.
Line 183: explicit test fixture `<local-command-caveat>text</local-command-caveat>` asserts the skip behaviour is intentional and covered by tests.

PR #106 removed an inactivity timer specifically because it was a flicker source. Treating shell-escape as a turn would create the same flicker every time the user runs `!ls`; the current skip is the conservative answer.

Section 'Shell escape command' (line 239-244): Expected bullets are `ready → working → ready` (structural) followed by 'state does not stick in working' (failure-mode). The two readings split on whether the structural bullet is normative or descriptive.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
claude is launched in interactive TUI mode so the ! shell-escape syntax is available
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/

script.keys: ! e c h o Space h e l l o Enter

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: alive

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready after launch
during the ~5 s window after the !echo shell-escape, NO state_transition to working fires for the UUID (the parser must skip <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrappers)
during that window, NO state_transition to waiting fires
after the normal user prompt, events.jsonl shows ready→working→ready for the UUID
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains the shell-escape wrapper lines (<bash-input> / <bash-stdout>) plus 1 user line and at least 1 assistant line for the final turn

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Scenario

Scenario-meanings `shell-escape-command`: the agent runs a shell command outside the formal tool framework; irrlicht stays `working` during execution and resolves to `ready` when the command exits. User-observable signal: `ready → working → ready`, no stick in `working`.

## agent_supports: yes

Codex 0.130.0's TUI has a user-typed shell escape. The binary string is literal: "Prefix a command with `!` to run it locally. Example: `!ls`" (strings scan of the darwin-arm64 vendor binary). Typed empirically in a tmux probe: `!ls` renders "• You ran ls" and `!sleep 5` renders "• Running sleep 5" then "• Working (1s • esc to interrupt)" for the full 5 s, then "• You ran sleep 5". So the TUI itself holds the session in a working/interruptible state for the entire command runtime and returns to the prompt when it exits — the exact `ready → working → ready` shape the scenario describes, with no LLM round-trip.

This is a genuinely distinct affordance from `full-lifecycle-toolcall`: that scenario exercises the model's `exec`/`function_call` tool (assistant decides to run a command); this one is a USER-initiated `!`-prefix shell escape with no model turn. The rollout proves the difference — a `!`-escape writes NO `function_call`/`function_call_output`, only a `task_started`, a synthetic `user_message` (role=user) whose content is a `<user_shell_command><command>…</command><result>Exit code: 0…</result></user_shell_command>` block, and a `task_complete`.

## daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

The daemon (FilesUnderRoot watch on `~/.codex/sessions`, codex parser) DOES observe a `ready → working → ready` arc for a `!`-escape, with no stuck-working and no premature-ready — the load-bearing intent of the scenario. Confirmed against the live recording daemon (pid 98544) for the probe session: two clean `state_transition` pairs (ready→working `force ready→working on first activity`, working→ready `agent finished turn → ready`), one per `!`-escape, and a terminal `process_exited`.

The gap that makes this `partial` rather than `yes`: the working DWELL the daemon records does NOT cover the command's actual runtime. Codex writes `task_started` to the rollout the instant the command begins, but the codex parser skips `task_started` (parser.go:155-172 emits only `task_complete` as `turn_done`); the only activity the parser turns into a state change is the synthetic `user_message`, which codex does not flush until the command FINISHES. For `!sleep 5` the rollout showed `task_started` at T+0, then `user_message`+`task_complete` together at T+5s. The daemon therefore transitioned ready→working only at command-completion time and settled back to ready ~2 s later (its debounce window), so the recorded working arc is debounce-bounded (~2 s) rather than command-bounded (~5 s). Topology and final state are correct and observable; the literal "stays working FOR THE DURATION" is not, because the parser has no event to anchor the working start to until the command is already done.

## Capability anchor

Primitive exercised = shell-escape mechanism (Claude Code `!cmd`, or analogous). Codex's analogue is the TUI `!`-prefix, anchored to `capabilities.json -> features.tui: true` (the escape is a TUI-only affordance; codex `exec` headless has no interactive `!` shell mode).
caveats: Working dwell is debounce-bounded (~2 s), not command-bounded: the codex parser skips task_started, and codex only flushes the synthetic user_message when the command exits, so the daemon cannot anchor the working-start to the command-start. Topology (ready→working→ready) and final state are correct; the literal 'stays working for the whole command' is not directly observable. This is the feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant pattern applied to a timing detail.
The !-escape is TUI-only; codex exec (headless) has no interactive ! shell mode. The scenario's requires:[headless_mode] is a scenario-level field; this cell is recorded interactively via tmux.

Unlike claudecode (whose parser deliberately skips <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> so state stays ready throughout), codex's !-escape DOES synthesize a turn (task_started/user_message/task_complete) and DOES drive a ready→working→ready arc.

sources.note: event_msg/task_complete → turn_done; task_started and other event_msg payloads skipped (lines 155-172); user/developer message → user_message with ClearToolNames
rule 4 default 'transcript activity → working'; rule 2b 'agent finished turn → ready' on turn_done — the two transitions observed for the probe
features.tui: true — the capability anchor for the !-prefix shell escape
strings scan: 'Prefix a command with ! to run it locally. Example: !ls'
!ls => task_started/user_message(<user_shell_command>)/task_complete same-ms; !sleep 5 => task_started at T+0, user_message+task_complete at T+5s, no function_call

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (codex 0.130.0; the !-prefix shell escape is a TUI affordance, confirmed in-binary: 'Prefix a command with ! to run it locally. Example: !ls')
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
codex is launched in interactive TUI mode (--no-alt-screen) so the ! shell-escape syntax is available — codex exec headless has no interactive ! shell mode
setup: Driver boots a codex TUI in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits out the Booting MCP phase
Codex assigns its own rollout UUID; the driver discovers the rollout-*.jsonl on the first wait_turn
recipe.recipe_notes: Each !cmd escape is typed as a keys step WITHOUT Enter, then a 1s sleep, then a separate keys:'Enter' step. The codex driver's keys handler (unlike step_send) does not pause before Enter, so a combined '! l s Enter' raced codex's Ink input render — Enter was silently dropped, the escapes accumulated in the input box, and the final send submitted ls/sleep/prompt as ONE shell command (collapsed into a single <user_shell_command>). Splitting typing from Enter with a render-settle sleep submits each escape on its own. The 9s sleep after !sleep 5 covers the command runtime plus the daemon settle.

script.keys: ! s l e e p Space 5

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: alive

verify: events.jsonl shows the codex rollout session entering ready after launch

each !cmd shell escape drives a ready→working→ready arc for the session (codex synthesizes a turn: task_started / user_message<user_shell_command> / task_complete) — UNLIKE claudecode, which skips the wrappers and stays ready
no state_transition gets stuck in working: every working transition is followed by a working→ready before the next escape or the final prompt
after the normal user prompt, events.jsonl shows ready→working→ready for the session, proving it is still alive
final session state is ready

NOTE (overlap + caveat): this is a USER !-escape, distinct from full-lifecycle-toolcall's model-driven exec tool (no function_call/function_call_output is written for a !-escape). The recorded working DWELL is debounce-bounded (~2 s), not command-bounded: codex's parser skips task_started and codex flushes the synthetic user_message only when the command exits, so the daemon cannot anchor working-start to command-start (see assessment.json — irrlicht_observes: partial). The spec therefore asserts the ready→working→ready topology and final ready, not a dwell length.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: unknown (leaning no) — opencode has NO user-level shell-ESCAPE primitive.
daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a. STOP / applicable_false.

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: unknown (leaning no) — opencode has NO user-level shell-ESCAPE primitive.
daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a. STOP / applicable_false.

## Scenario

shell-escape-command asserts a USER-TYPED shell ESCAPE: a `!cmd`-style prefix (claudecode `!echo hello`, codex `!ls`, pi `!echo hi`) or aider's `/run <cmd>` alias `!<cmd>` that runs a shell command LOCALLY, OUTSIDE a model turn, as a distinct local-exec event — no LLM round-trip at escape time. The cell-distinguishing signal is precisely that the command is dispatched by the USER (not the model) and runs as its own local-exec event; how the parser then treats the resulting wrapper (claudecode/pi skip it → stay ready; codex synthesizes an arc; aider folds it into a turn-state arc with no tool_result) is per-adapter, but every sibling row has a real `!cmd`/`/run` escape primitive to drive.

## Why opencode has no such primitive (agent_supports: unknown/no)

opencode exposes shell access ONLY as the FORMAL `bash` TOOL invoked BY THE MODEL inside a turn — not as a user-typed `!cmd` escape that runs outside a model turn. There is no `!`-prefix REPL affordance and no `/run`-style local-exec command that bypasses the LLM. The model-invoked bash tool is already covered by the recordable opencode/auto-executed-tool-call cell (gemma's tool running inside the working span, ready→working→ready). So the user-level shell-ESCAPE the scenario asserts simply does not exist in opencode: there is nothing distinct from auto-executed-tool-call to elicit, and capabilities.json declares no shell_escape feature (only tool_calls:true — the model-invoked tool). Marked agent_supports:unknown rather than a hard no because opencode's TUI affordances were assessed from the headless `opencode run` path and the adapter source, not exhaustively from the live TUI; but no `!cmd`-style user escape is documented or surfaced, and the recording rig has no way to drive one.

## Why not recordable (daemon/driver n/a)

Per the #476 routing decision, agent_supports ∈ {unknown} routes to applicable_false — STOP, no recipe, no recording, no live CLI. daemon_capability and driver_capability are n/a because there is no behavior to observe or drive: the daemon side is moot (a model-invoked bash tool is the auto-executed-tool-call arc, not a shell escape), and the headless `opencode run` driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh, case "$TYPE" handles only send|wait_turn|sleep) has no escape / `!` / `keys` step to issue a user shell escape — but that driver gap is downstream of the agent gap, not the blocker, so this is NOT a driver_gap verdict.

## Confidence

0.8: opencode's shell-as-model-tool model is read directly from the adapter parser (type:"tool" parts only) and capabilities.json (no shell_escape feature, tool_calls:true is the model-invoked tool); the auto-executed-tool-call recording proves the model-invoked bash tool is already the covered shape. Not higher because the live TUI was not exhaustively probed for an undocumented `!`-style affordance — hence agent_supports:unknown rather than a hard no.
caveats: opencode shell access is the model-invoked `bash` TOOL, not a user-typed `!cmd` shell ESCAPE. The model-invoked tool is already covered by the recordable opencode/auto-executed-tool-call cell; there is nothing distinct for this scenario to elicit.

agent_supports:unknown (not a hard no) because the live TUI was not exhaustively probed for an undocumented `!`-style affordance — but none is documented, capabilities.json declares no shell_escape feature, and the headless `opencode run` driver has no escape step.

This is applicable_false (agent_supports:unknown → STOP per #476), NOT driver_gap: the headless driver's lack of an escape/`!`/keys step is downstream of the agent gap — there is no user-level shell-escape primitive to drive in the first place.

sources.note: shell access surfaces only as type:"tool" parts (parseToolPart, parser.go:199-226) — the MODEL-invoked bash tool, tracked as a tool call inside a turn. No user-typed shell-escape / local-exec branch exists.
declares tool_calls:true (the model-invoked tool) and NO shell_escape feature — opencode has no user-level `!cmd` escape that runs outside a model turn.

the recordable sibling that ALREADY covers opencode's shell access: the model-invoked bash/write tool running auto-executed inside the working span (ready→working→ready). A shell-escape recording would either be indistinguishable from this or have nothing to drive.
headless `opencode run` driver: case "$TYPE" handles only send|wait_turn|sleep — no escape / `!` / keys step. (Downstream of the agent gap, not the blocker.)

the scenario's sibling recipes all drive a real user `!cmd`/`/run` escape: claudecode `!echo hello` (keys), codex `!ls`/`!sleep 5` (keys), pi `!echo hi` (send), aider `/run echo hello` (slash). opencode has no analog primitive.

recipe.notes: scope_note: opencode has no user-level shell-escape (`!cmd`) that runs outside a model turn; shell is the model-invoked bash tool, already covered by auto-executed-tool-call. agent_supports:unknown (per assessment.json) → applicable_false (#476): no recipe script, no recording. The headless `opencode run` driver also has no escape/`!` step, but that gap is downstream of the missing agent primitive (not a driver_gap).

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Pi supports a native shell escape: any interactive-REPL input beginning with `!` is run as a local shell command WITHOUT an LLM round-trip (verified live against pi 0.70.0 — interactive-mode.js line 2079 `if (text.startsWith("!"))` dispatches to handleBashCommand, which calls session.executeBash and recordBashResult; a leading `!!` additionally excludes the output from LLM context, per the startup banner hint 'rawKeyHint("!!", "to run bash (no context)")'). Pi records the escape as a `{"type":"message","message":{"role":"bashExecution",...}}` line in the rollout transcript — a user-side wrapper, NOT an assistant turn and NOT a formal toolCall/toolResult pair.

The scenario's literal spec wording (scenario-meanings.md Conceptual flow) is ready→working→ready: state transitions to working while the shell command runs, then back to ready. irrlicht does NOT reproduce that literal arc, because the pi parser (core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go:128-131) explicitly matches the `bashExecution` role and SKIPS it (`ev.Skip = true`) — confirmed by parser_test.go which asserts bashExecution is skipped and that a trailing bashExecution leaves LastEventType==turn_done unchanged. So no transcript_activity is recorded for the escape and no state_transition to working fires; the session stays ready across the shell escape.

irrlicht_observes is therefore `partial` (not `yes`): the daemon does NOT synthesize the working→ready arc the literal spec describes. What it DOES guarantee — and what this cell asserts as the intent reading — is the safety invariant that matters for a session-monitor: the shell escape does NOT leave the session stuck in working (no spurious working that never resolves) and does NOT emit a spurious waiting; the session remains ready and a subsequent real prompt still drives a clean ready→working→ready arc, proving the session is alive and uncorrupted. The alternative (literal) reading — synthesize a transition arc for the bash escape — would require a parser change to stop skipping bashExecution, which is out of scope for a recording.

Confidence 0.75: the supported behavior and the parser-skip are both directly verified in source + live binary, but the observable is a NEGATIVE invariant over the escape window (no working, no waiting, no stuck state) plus a positive real-prompt arc before it — strong but indirect proof that the escape was harmless.

WHY a warm-up real turn FIRST (before the escape): (1) pi creates its rollout-*.jsonl only after the first user message, so the driver's resolve_transcript needs a real turn to bind the UUID; (2) it supplies the one legitimate ready→working→ready arc that proves a NORMAL prompt still drives state, in contrast to the escape's silence.
caveats: irrlicht_observes is `partial`: the daemon does NOT reproduce the literal ready→working→ready arc the scenario-meanings Conceptual flow describes for the shell command — the pi parser deliberately skips the bashExecution wrapper (parser.go:128-131). The cell adopts the INTENT reading: the escape must not leave the session stuck in working and must not emit waiting; state stays ready across the escape. The literal arc would require a parser change.

Shell-escape syntax is a leading `!` (verified pi 0.70.0: interactive-mode.js handleBashCommand). `!echo hi` runs locally with no LLM round-trip and writes a single bashExecution rollout line. `!!` is the no-context variant; either is skipped identically by the pi parser.

The observable for the escape itself is purely NEGATIVE (no second working, no waiting, no stuck state) — 'a shell escape was issued' is invisible to events.jsonl because the bashExecution wrapper is skipped. The warm-up real turn provides the positive ready→working→ready arc to contrast against the escape's silence.

The live REPL may briefly emit a proc-<PID> pre-session born ready before the UUID-keyed rollout transcript appears; the spec anchors session_birth to the pre-session-tolerant 'ready within 2s' match and pins the warm-up arc + post-escape dwell to the rollout via same_session_as (turn_start anchored to recording start with no pin binds the FIRST working — necessarily the rollout, since the pre-session never goes working).

sources.note: Lines 128-131: case "bashExecution" sets ev.Skip = true and returns — the user-side shell escape (! command) is deliberately skipped, so no state machinery fires and the session stays ready.

Lines 106-111 assert a bashExecution message is skipped; lines 482-505 assert that a bashExecution after an assistant stopReason=stop leaves LastEventType==turn_done unchanged (no working synthesized).

Line 2079 `if (text.startsWith("!"))` routes the input to handleBashCommand (line 4427) → session.executeBash + recordBashResult, with no LLM round-trip; `!!` (line 2080) excludes output from context. Startup banner advertises `!!` 'to run bash (no context)'. messages.js records role:"bashExecution" with command + output fields.

Interactive tmux REPL driver supporting send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep; a `send` step types `!echo hi` + Enter inside the live REPL to trigger the shell escape (no wait_turn after, since the escape produces no assistant stopReason=stop turn).

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (PI_API_KEY or configured provider; verified against pi 0.70.0)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
pi is launched in interactive REPL mode so the ! shell-escape syntax is available — headless `pi --print` is single-shot and has no interactive ! shell mode

INTENT READING (per assessment.json — irrlicht_observes: partial): the pi parser deliberately SKIPS the bashExecution wrapper (parser.go:128-131), so irrlicht does NOT reproduce the literal ready→working→ready arc the scenario-meanings Conceptual flow describes for the shell command. This cell asserts the safety invariant instead — the escape must not leave the session stuck in working and must not emit waiting; state stays ready across the escape. The literal arc would require a parser change.
setup: Driver boots a fresh pi REPL in a per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/; pi assigns its own session UUID per rollout

The shell escape is issued via a `send` step typing the literal text `!echo hi` + Enter (the pi driver's send handler uses tmux send-keys -l, so the leading ! is sent verbatim and pi runs it as a local shell command); NO wait_turn follows the escape, since `!`-prefixed input produces a bashExecution rollout line, not an assistant stopReason=stop turn

Avoid !! only if you want context inclusion to differ — either ! or !! is skipped identically by the pi parser; this recipe uses single ! (output retained in context, no LLM round-trip at escape time)

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working→ready cycle for the entire run, driven by the warm-up turn only (pi's first message drives working, stopReason=stop drives ready); pi may flush the single warm-up turn as one OR two working→ready bursts

events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to working after the warm-up turn returns to ready — the !echo shell escape drives none because the pi parser skips the bashExecution wrapper (parser.go:128-131)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point
the escape window (≥6s after the warm-up turn ends) shows the rollout session continuously ready — no transcript_activity is recorded for the !echo escape; the session is NOT left stuck in working

transcript.jsonl contains 1 user message line, 1 assistant stopReason=stop line (the warm-up turn), and 1 bashExecution line (the !echo escape — which does NOT appear as an assistant turn and is skipped by the parser)
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session; final session state is ready
recipe.notes: Pi difference vs claudecode/codex: pi runs `!`-prefixed REPL input as a LOCAL shell command with no LLM round-trip (verified live against pi 0.70.0 — interactive-mode.js text.startsWith("!") → handleBashCommand → executeBash + recordBashResult; the `!!` variant additionally excludes output from context). Pi records the escape as ONE {"type":"message","message":{"role":"bashExecution",...}} rollout line. The pi parser (core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go:128-131) deliberately SKIPS the bashExecution role (ev.Skip = true), so — UNLIKE codex, which synthesizes a working→ready arc for its !-escape, and LIKE claudecode, which skips its <bash-input> wrappers — the daemon sees no transcript_activity and the session stays ready across the escape. The whole observable is the ABSENCE of a second working transition plus the proof (warm-up arc) that a NORMAL prompt still drives state. RECIPE SHAPE: the warm-up turn comes FIRST (the escape SECOND) — opposite to the claudecode recipe — because pi creates ~/.pi/agent/sessions/.../<ts>_<uuid>.jsonl only after the first user message, so without a leading real turn the driver's resolve_transcript never binds a UUID. The escape uses `send` (not the claudecode `keys` step): the pi interactive driver supports send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep but NOT keys, and step_send types the literal text (-l) then Enter, so `!echo hi` is delivered verbatim. NO wait_turn after the escape: it produces no assistant stopReason=stop, so wait_turn would time out; the trailing 6s sleep instead holds the recording open past the classifier debounce so the post-escape ready dwell is observed cleanly and the daemon-kill doesn't race the final-state emission. C7 / SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch (bridges the gap until the UUID rollout transcript appears); session_birth legitimately matches that pre-session, while the warm-up arc and post-escape dwell pin to the rollout via same_session_as (turn_start anchors to recording start with NO pin so the matcher binds the FIRST working — necessarily the rollout, since the pre-session never goes working). Between runs: warm-up assistant wording, token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ, and pi may flush the warm-up turn as one or two working→ready bursts; the load-bearing assertion is the silent ready dwell across the escape window. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go (case "bashExecution" → ev.Skip) and whether a pi update changed how `!` escapes are recorded in the rollout.
